summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Earth's Layers (Crust, Mantle, Core)
summary_sections.content_html:
Earth has four main layers, each with distinct properties:

  • Crust: the thin, rocky outer layer (5–70 km thick). Oceanic crust is denser (basalt); continental crust is thicker and less dense (granite).
  • Mantle: the thickest layer (~2,900 km). The upper mantle contains the partially molten asthenosphere, which allows tectonic plates to move.
  • Outer Core: liquid iron and nickel (~2,200 km thick). Convection here generates Earth's magnetic field.
  • Inner Core: solid iron and nickel (~1,200 km radius), kept solid by immense pressure despite extreme temperatures (~5,400 °C).

Scientists study Earth's interior using seismic waves—P-waves travel through solids and liquids; S-waves travel only through solids.
